texts.label: text
texts.orig: Application of bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED) for simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron from aqueous solution was investigated using Astom Acilyzer EX3B model ED device. In the laboratory scale experiments performed, e ff ects of acid and base solutions types and their concentrations employed in acid and base compartments, electrical potential applied on process e ffi ciency were investigated. The results showed that separations and recoveries of both boron and lithium were e ff ective when 0.05 M HCl and 0.05 M NaOH solutions have been used in acid and base compartments, respectively. The percent separations of boron and lithium were 86.9% and 94.7%, respectively, while their respective recoveries were 50% and 62% at 30 V. The maximum separation and recovery values for boron and lithium were obtained when a 30 V of electrical potential was applied. With optimal initial acid and base concentrations (0.05 M) used in acid and base compartments, the speci fi c power consumption (SPC) was calculated as 7.9 kWh/m 3 at 30 V.
texts.text: Application of bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED) for simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron from aqueous solution was investigated using Astom Acilyzer EX3B model ED device. In the laboratory scale experiments performed, e ff ects of acid and base solutions types and their concentrations employed in acid and base compartments, electrical potential applied on process e ffi ciency were investigated. The results showed that separations and recoveries of both boron and lithium were e ff ective when 0.05 M HCl and 0.05 M NaOH solutions have been used in acid and base compartments, respectively. The percent separations of boron and lithium were 86.9% and 94.7%, respectively, while their respective recoveries were 50% and 62% at 30 V. The maximum separation and recovery values for boron and lithium were obtained when a 30 V of electrical potential was applied. With optimal initial acid and base concentrations (0.05 M) used in acid and base compartments, the speci fi c power consumption (SPC) was calculated as 7.9 kWh/m 3 at 30 V.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While higher initial acid and base concentrations showed higher boron and lithium separation e ffi ciencies, relatively lower concentrations of acid and base solutions employed in acid and base compartments improved the recoveries of boron and lithium. Acid and base solutions generating a moderate conductive medium in acid and base compartments improved separations along with recoveries of boron and lithium from solution. When the solutions of 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH were used in acid and base compartments, recoveries of boron and lithium were 27% and 33%, respectively. On the other hand, the recoveries of boron and lithium were 62% and 56%, respectively when 0.1 M H3BO3 and 0.1 M LiOH solutions were employed in acid and base compartments.

texts.orig: Boron is a vital element as nutrient for plants, animals and humans [9]. Before 2011, World Health Organization (WHO) declared that the permissible concentration of boron should be 0.5 mg/L in drinking water. But this value was revised in 2011 as 2.4 mg/L due to the positive e ff ects of boron for human health [10]. Tolerable limit of boron is di ff erent according to the kinds of plants and maximum boron tolerance limit changes from 0.5 mg/L to 10 mg/L. These limit values depend much on boron tolerance of the plant. Exposure of plants to high concentrations of boron leads to yield loss, inhibition of plant growth and/ or may cause death of plant [11].

texts.orig: Boron and lithium can exist in geothermal waters and seawater [9,12]. The total amount of lithium in the oceans is estimated as 230 -240 billion tonnes [12]. Thus, recovery of boron and lithium from natural waters such as geothermal water and seawater has attracted the interest of scientists recently [4,13]. It has been reported that boron can be separated from water by using reverse osmosis (RO) and ion exchange methods. The RO process can be applied for removal of boron from water although a polishing step for product water is needed after RO step to remove excess boron. Ion exchange resins can be used for removal and recovery of boron, but this method has some drawbacks also [14].

Ion exchange resins can be used for removal and recovery of boron, but this method has some drawbacks also [14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the literature, there are various methods applied for lithium removal from aqueous solutions including adsorption, ion-exchange, bioaccumulation, membrane processes [12]. For instance, MnO2 adsorbents packed in naylon-mesh bags were used for removal of lithium from sea water. These bags were put into di ff erent depths in sea and 14 -15 mg Li/g adsorbent was obtained after the 58 days of operation [15]. Additionally, solvent extraction method was also employed for recovery of lithium from lithium ion batteries. However, solvent extraction was de fi ned as costly and energy-intensive process [3].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED) is a method which has high e ffi ciency to produce acid and base from the salt solution [16]. This process includes three types of membranes which are cation exchange, anion exchange and bipolar membranes. When a direct electrical potential is applied, water molecules present in hydrophilic part between cation and anion exchange layers of bipolar membranes are split into H + and OH -ions. Anions and cations of salt solution passing through anion and cation exchange membranes combine with H + and OH -ions to produce a separate mixture of corresponding acid and base, respectively [17]. It is very crucial that bipolar membranes have high permselectivity, low electrical resistance, high rate of water splitting, low voltage drop, high current e ffi ciency, good chemical and mechanical stability and long life-time. Salt leakage due to the co-ion migration can be prevented by the help of high permselectivity of bipolar membrane. Furthermore, salt leakage that resulting from the coion migration can be prevented if bipolar membranes have a high permselectivity [18]. Generally, a pre-treatment process is recommended before BMED process to ensure that the membranes are not blocked by divalent ions in the aqueous solution. Also, acid and base produced should have high purity at the end of BMED process. Ion exchange and selective adsorption processes could be used as pretreatment steps for BMED process when model solutions are not used [17,19].

This process includes three types of membranes which are cation exchange, anion exchange and bipolar membranes. When a direct electrical potential is applied, water molecules present in hydrophilic part between cation and anion exchange layers of bipolar membranes are split into H + and OH -ions. Anions and cations of salt solution passing through anion and cation exchange membranes combine with H + and OH -ions to produce a separate mixture of corresponding acid and base, respectively [17]. It is very crucial that bipolar membranes have high permselectivity, low electrical resistance, high rate of water splitting, low voltage drop, high current e ffi ciency, good chemical and mechanical stability and long life-time. Salt leakage due to the co-ion migration can be prevented by the help of high permselectivity of bipolar membrane. Furthermore, salt leakage that resulting from the coion migration can be prevented if bipolar membranes have a high permselectivity [18]. Generally, a pre-treatment process is recommended before BMED process to ensure that the membranes are not blocked by divalent ions in the aqueous solution. Also, acid and base produced should have high purity at the end of BMED process. Ion exchange and selective adsorption processes could be used as pretreatment steps for BMED process when model solutions are not used [17,19].

texts.orig: 2. Experimental
texts.text: 2. Experimental

texts.label: text
texts.orig: BMED experiments were carried out with a laboratory scale Astom Acilyzer EX3B model electrodialyzer. The BMED stack con fi guration and assembly was described by Bunani et al. [20]. The properties of membranes installed in the BMED stack were given in Table 1. Flow sheet for removal and recovery of boron and lithium by BMED was illustrated in Fig. 1 [20]. A 2.0 L of model solution of lithium tetraborate (Li2B4O7·5H2O) was employed in sample compartment. The solution contains 1000 mg B/L and 340 mg Li/L. This model solution represents the composition of the typical eluate obtained from ion exchange pre-treatment step of a typical geothermal water in Japan. Acid and base compartments were fed with 200 mL of HCl and NaOH solutions at various concentrations (0.1, 0.05 and 0.005 M). In other tests, acid and base compartments were fi lled with 200 mL of H3BO3 and LiOH solutions, respectively at two di ff erent concentrations (0.05 and 0.1 M). The electrical potentials applied were 15, 20 and 30 V. In all experiments, a 500 mL of 0.1 M NaOH solution was circulated in the electrode compartment.

Acid and base compartments were fed with 200 mL of HCl and NaOH solutions at various concentrations (0.1, 0.05 and 0.005 M). In other tests, acid and base compartments were fi lled with 200 mL of H3BO3 and LiOH solutions, respectively at two di ff erent concentrations (0.05 and 0.1 M). The electrical potentials applied were 15, 20 and 30 V. In all experiments, a 500 mL of 0.1 M NaOH solution was circulated in the electrode compartment.

Based on such result obtained, it was considered that all the transferred ions did not reach the recovery compartments because of the amount of some ions retained in or on the membrane. Removals of boron and lithium from the sample solution were more than their recoveries in acid and base compartments, respectively. As it can be seen in Table 2 and Fig. 3, a 86.7% of boron was removed from sample solution while only a 50% of boron was recovered in acid compartment with optimal initial acid and base concentrations of 0.05 M. However, according to the material balance, recovery values of boron and lithium are expected to be higher. Such results may be resulted from ion-retention on the membrane surface. This situation can be prevented by increasing experiment period or by using di ff erent ion exchange membranes. It was reported that adsorption of ions on membrane surface depends on characteristics of membranes employed. Iizuka et al. [3] reported about such phenomenon due to the lithium ion retained by cation exchange membranes. The authors proposed a semi-batch operation to prevent ion retention on the membrane surface.

texts.orig: The speci fi c power consumptions (SPC) were calculated for the experiments run with various initial acid and base concentrations used in acid and base compartments. When higher initial acid and base concentrations were employed, speci fi c power consumption was also high. Using high initial acid and base concentrations in acid and base compartments leads to an increase in the integrated current. Therefore, the optimal initial acid and base concentrations for both boron and lithium separation and recovery e ffi ciencies with reasonable SPC by using BMED were 0.05 M at 30 V of electrical potential (Fig. 4).

texts.orig: In this study, in order to investigate the e ff ect of electrical potential on BMED, di ff erent electrical potentials (15 V, 20 V, 30 V) were applied. During these studies, the acid and base solutions in related compartments were 0.05 M HCl and 0.05 M NaOH, respectively. The recoveries of lithium and boron versus time in the base and acid compartments were shown in Figs. 5 and 6, respectively. Maximum removals of lithium and boron from the sample solution were 94.7% and 86.9%, respectively under 30 V of electrical potential (Table 2). Removals and recoveries of lithium and boron increased with increasing electrical potential. Recoveries of boron in acid compartment were calculated as 12.3%, 29.0% and 49.9% at 15 V, 20 V, and 30 V, respectively. On the other hand, respective recoveries of lithium were 25%, 31% and 62% at 15 V, 20 V, 30 V.

texts.orig: As obtained here, even if the electrical potential is increased, removal and recovery of boron and lithium cannot be achieved as equal. One of the reasons for this phenomenon is the partial transfer of B (OH)4 -ions to the base compartment through cation exchange membrane and the partial transfer of Li + ions to acid compartment through anion exchange membrane. Bunani et al. [19] also reported about ion leakage during their study on recovery of lithium and boron by using BMED. They mentioned that small amount of boron in base compartment and the presence of lithium in acid compartment were detected [19].

texts.orig: Elsewhere, the e ff ect of electrical potential on recoveries of boron and lithium from aqueous solution simultaneously by BMED was reported [20]. It was found that concentration of lithium and boron ions in acid and base compartments increased when applied electrical potential was high [20]. In our studies, the maximum recoveries of lithium and boron were obtained at 30 V of electrical potential as it can be seen in Figs. 5 and 6. Similar results were reported by Bunani et al. [20] also. It was mentioned that applying an electrical potential over 30 V would never improve the ions recovery but leads to higher SPC only [23].

texts.orig: From the results of the present work, it can be concluded that electrical potential which is < 30 V is not enough to achieve higher separation and recovery e ffi ciencies with the same model of BMED system employed by Bunani et al [20] On the other hand, in consequence of increasing of the electrical potential, SPC increased also. The SPC values calculated were 1.6 kWh/m 3 , 3.3 kWh/m 3 , and 7.9 kWh/m 3 at 15 V, 20 V and 30 V of electrical potentials, respectively (Fig. 7).

texts.orig: Without considering whether the acid and base solutions are weak or strong electrolytes, the conductivities of the solutions employed in acid and base compartments should be in the range between 300 and 500 μ S/cm to get better separation and recovery e ffi ciencies with BMED system [23]. Lower conductivities of the solutions in acid and base compartments will increase the resistance of the medium which results in lower e ffi ciency in recovery. On the other hand, due to the back di ff usion of B(OH)4 -and Li + ions, solutions with higher conductivities result in lower e ffi ciencies in recovery as well.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The initial concentrations of acid and base solutions employed in acid and base compartments should be carefully selected.
texts.text: The initial concentrations of acid and base solutions employed in acid and base compartments should be carefully selected.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The electrical potential applied is an important parameter in BMED process. Lower electrical potential than the optimal one is not enough to achieve a good performance of the process. In this study, three di ff erent electrical potentials were tested and 30 V was found as optimal electrical potential because of the fact that higher boron and lithium recovery values were obtained at this voltage. However, optimal electrical potential may change with applying di ff erent processes conditions. For example, when di ff erent ion exchange membranes and di ff erent initial concentrations of boron and lithium in the sample solution were used, optimal electrical potential may be probably di ff erent.
texts.text: The electrical potential applied is an important parameter in BMED process. Lower electrical potential than the optimal one is not enough to achieve a good performance of the process. In this study, three di ff erent electrical potentials were tested and 30 V was found as optimal electrical potential because of the fact that higher boron and lithium recovery values were obtained at this voltage. However, optimal electrical potential may change with applying di ff erent processes conditions. For example, when di ff erent ion exchange membranes and di ff erent initial concentrations of boron and lithium in the sample solution were used, optimal electrical potential may be probably di ff erent.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The choice of initial concentrations of acid and base solutions in acid and base compartments should be based on the nature and/or the types of the acid/base solutions employed. Weak acids and bases required higher concentrations and strong ones required moderately lower concentrations. For instance, when H3BO3 is used as initial acid instead of HCl, the concentration of H3BO3 should be increased to obtain a higher boron recovery in acid compartment.
texts.text: The choice of initial concentrations of acid and base solutions in acid and base compartments should be based on the nature and/or the types of the acid/base solutions employed. Weak acids and bases required higher concentrations and strong ones required moderately lower concentrations. For instance, when H3BO3 is used as initial acid instead of HCl, the concentration of H3BO3 should be increased to obtain a higher boron recovery in acid compartment.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Regardless to the nature and/or the types of the acid and base solutions employed in acid and base compartments, the initial electrical conductivities of the solutions in the acid and base compartments are more important than the initial concentrations of acid and base solutions used.
texts.text: Regardless to the nature and/or the types of the acid and base solutions employed in acid and base compartments, the initial electrical conductivities of the solutions in the acid and base compartments are more important than the initial concentrations of acid and base solutions used.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Boron and lithium recoveries can be also increased by preventing ion retention on the membrane surface. The use of di ff erent ion exchange membranes that have lower electrical resistance can be used in order to increase boron and lithium recoveries and to decrease ion retentions on the membrane surface.

The use of di ff erent ion exchange membranes that have lower electrical resistance can be used in order to increase boron and lithium recoveries and to decrease ion retentions on the membrane surface.

texts.orig: · The SPC was in fl uenced either by the concentrations and the types of acid and base solutions used in the acid and base compartments. The electrical potential applied was also important parameter affecting SPC. Increasing electrical potential can raise the recovery values of boron and lithium; however, it will also increase the SPC value.
